questions.q: Which country has the largest number of native Spanish speakers?
Spanish evolved primarily from which form of Latin?
In how many countries is Spanish an official language?
What is the formal 'you' pronoun in Spanish?
Which famous Spanish author is the language sometimes nicknamed after?
What is the mixed language spoken in border villages between Portugal and Spanish-speaking regions called?
How many tenses do Spanish verbs have in their basic forms?
What is the Spanish-based creole language spoken in Mindanao, Philippines?
Who authored the first modern European grammar of Spanish in 1492?
Spanish is classified as what type of language regarding motion expression?
During the Visigothic period in Spain, Spanish borrowed words from which Germanic language?
What linguistic term describes the use of 'vos' instead of 'tú' in Argentina?
Approximately how many proficient Spanish speakers remain in the Philippines?
Which U.S. territory officially uses Spanish alongside English?
Spanish ranks as the fourth most spoken language worldwide after English, Mandarin, and what?
In Spanish, if a word ends in 'n', 's', or a vowel with no accent mark, where does the stress fall?
What phonological process changed Latin 'annum' to Spanish 'año'?
Spanish belongs to which major language family?
How many official vowel sounds does Spanish have?
Spanish is primarily derived from which ancient language?
What is the approximate percentage of Spanish vocabulary with Latin origins?
In which international organization is Spanish one of the six official languages?
What is the name of the other term used for the Spanish language within Spain and parts of Latin America?
Which African nation is the only country where Spanish is the sole official language?
What grammatical feature allows Spanish speakers to omit subject pronouns?
What is the linguistic phenomenon where /ʎ/ and /ʝ/ merge in most Spanish-speaking regions?
In which country is Spanish co-official alongside Quechua and Aymara?
Approximately how many native Spanish speakers were recorded between 2022 and 2024?
Which city's media and speech patterns established the standard for European Spanish pronunciation?
Spanish is classified within which branch of the Romance languages?
Equatorial Guinea is notable as which type of country in relation to Spanish?
choices: The smallest Spanish-speaking country in the Americas
The largest Spanish-speaking country in Asia
The newest Spanish-speaking country in Europe
The only entirely Spanish-speaking country in Africa

questions.q: Which medieval manuscript is a significant early record of Spanish language and culture?
Approximately how many words of Arabic origin are found in modern Spanish?
Which of these regions is traditionally associated with the earliest development of the Spanish language?
What term describes the distinction between formal 'usted' and informal 'tú' pronouns in Spanish?
Spanish ranks third for internet usage among world languages. Which language ranks second?
How many grammatical moods does Spanish include?
Which Spanish-speaking country uses 'ustedes' as the standard plural second-person pronoun in both formal and informal contexts?
What linguistic feature resulted from the Basque substratum influence on early Spanish phonology?
choices: The elimination of the rolled r
The aspiration of the Latin /f/ sound into /h/
The loss of all consonant clusters

questions.q: What are the three infinitive endings that Spanish verbs use?
According to 2020 U.S. census data, approximately how many residents of the United States speak Spanish at home?
What is the Spanish term that literally means 'the vocabulary' or 'the word stock'?
Which 9th-century manuscript, discovered in the Rioja region, contains some of the earliest written attestations of Romance language diverging from Latin?
Antonio de Nebrija's famous statement to Queen Isabella in 1492 claimed that language served what primary function for empire?
choices: The instrument of conquest and imperial consolidation

questions.q: In the phonological phenomenon known as the 'Reajuste de las sibilantes' during the 15th-16th centuries, what sound did the letters ⟨j⟩ and ⟨g⟩ before front vowels acquire?
Spanish evolved from Vulgar Latin, but in which specific Roman province of Iberia did this linguistic transformation primarily occur?
The term 'lengua de Cervantes' as a poetic epithet for Spanish originates from which of Cervantes' works gaining international prominence?
choices: Los trabajos de Persiles y Sigismunda

questions.q: In Spanish orthography, the acute accent mark historically served which primary function in word recognition?
choices: Marking irregular stress patterns that deviate from default rules

questions.q: Spanish exhibits a syllable-timed rhythmic pattern in speech. Which major European language family member shares this characteristic rather than stress-timing?
In northern Castilian Spanish, the pronunciation of ⟨s⟩ as an apico-alveolar [s̺] represents which type of articulatory distinction?
choices: A dialectal feature exclusive to Madrid speech
A phonemic distinction between two separate consonant sounds
A modern innovation adopted after the 20th century
A regional allophone with no phonemic significance

questions.q: Spanish contains approximately what percentage of its vocabulary derived from Arabic, a legacy of medieval Al-Andalus?
In linguistic typology, Spanish is classified as a verb-framed language. What does this designation indicate about motion expression?
choices: Verbs encode manner of motion, while paths are expressed as adverbials
Verbs encode the path of motion, while manner is expressed in satellites
Verbs cannot express motion without additional prepositional phrases
Verbs require double marking for both path and manner simultaneously

questions.q: The medieval manuscript 'Cantar de mio Cid' provides crucial evidence of early Spanish language development and is associated with which geographical region?
The process of lenition affecting Latin consonants in the development of Spanish primarily occurred in which phonological environment?
choices: At the end of words before consonants

questions.q: Which phonological process, occurring in the 15th-16th centuries, transformed Latin /ts/ and /dz/ sounds into the modern Spanish velar fricative /x/ represented by letters like ⟨j⟩?
The diacritical mark that modifies the pronunciation of ⟨g⟩ before e or i in Spanish orthography is called a what?
In the early 9th century, the Glosas Emilianenses documented the first systematic written evidence of Spanish in which northern Spanish city?
The phonetic process by which intervocalic consonants weaken—exemplified by Latin vīta becoming Spanish vida—is linguistically termed what?
Spanish derives etymologically from the Vulgar Latin term 'hispaniolus,' which literally meant what?
In 1973, which Philippine president briefly removed Spanish from official language status before restoring it the same year?
Since 2010, which two previously recognized single letters in Spanish collation have been officially reclassified as digraphs or letter pairs?
How many grammatically distinct conjugated forms does a typical Spanish verb exhibit across all tenses, moods, and persons?
The Association of Spanish Language Academies, which regulates Spanish language standards, is known by which Spanish acronym?
In medieval Spanish linguistic history, before the standardized term 'Castellano' gained widespread acceptance, the language was generically referred to as what?
Antonio de Nebrija's grammatical work, published in 1492, is often considered the first modern European grammar of which language?
The Real Academia Española coordinates language standards through an umbrella organization called the Association of Spanish Language Academies, founded in what year?
Chavacano, a Spanish-based creole language spoken primarily in Mindanao, developed through contact with which colonial power's traders?
In the Castilian Spanish dialect of northern Spain, the sound [s̺] is characterized by which articulatory feature?
The term 'portuñol' refers to a mixed language combining Spanish and which other language, commonly used in border regions?
Which Latin linguistic substrate, influenced by indigenous populations of the Iberian Peninsula, contributed to the aspiration of the Latin /f/ sound in early Spanish?
The collapse of which political entity in the 5th century CE set the conditions for the evolution of Latin dialects into Romance languages including Spanish?
In advanced Spanish grammar, which mood is typically used in subordinate clauses introduced by expressions of doubt, desire, or emotion?
The earliest systematic written records of the Spanish language appear in documents from which northern Spanish city during the 10th-11th centuries?
In Spanish linguistics, the phenomenon where speakers pronounce both 'z' and 'c' before 'e' and 'i' identically to the 's' sound is called what?
The International Organization for Standardization assigns which three-letter code to Spanish in the ISO 639-3 standard?
In Spanish historical linguistics, the development of the 'ñ' character from the medieval abbreviation of which double letter represents an early orthographic innovation?
Which West Iberian language, spoken in parts of Catalonia and Valencia, is co-official alongside Spanish in those regions?
The Spanish-based creole language Chavacano, spoken in Mindanao in the Philippines, exhibits influence from which other Romance language beyond Spanish?
In Spanish morphology, the palatalization of Latin geminate consonants is exemplified by the transformation of Latin annum into which Spanish word?
The Spanish letter ñ represents which specific phoneme in Spanish orthography?
Which Iberian microstate has Catalan, Spanish, and French as official or co-official languages due to its unique geographic and political position?
In Spanish morphosyntax, the use of reflexive clitic se in passive or impersonal constructions, as in se venden casas, is known as what construction type?
The term Portuñol refers to a mixed language or code-switching phenomenon occurring primarily between which two nations?
In Spanish grammar, which infinitive verb ending is associated with verbs like 'hablar' and 'cantar'?
Which Spanish grammarian from the 15th century famously stated that language is the instrument of empire to Queen Isabella?
Which regional trade bloc, established in the 1990s, significantly increased Spanish language instruction in Brazil?
In Spanish phonology, what diacritical mark indicates irregular stress placement on a syllable?
Which organization is responsible for establishing and regulating the standard norms of the Spanish language?
Which major world power outside Spain and Latin America has the largest population of Spanish speakers?
What linguistic classification describes Spanish as a language where each syllable receives approximately equal duration?
In the Canary Islands dialect, what linguistic feature describes the aspiration of syllable-final /s/ to a sound resembling [h]?
What pronoun serves as both formal and informal second-person plural throughout most of Hispanic America?
In Northern Spain phonology, which consonant sound is typically pronounced as an apico-alveolar sibilant [s̺]?
What script system does the Spanish language use for writing?
Which medieval manuscript is credited with recording the earliest written traces of the Castilian language?
In what century do the earliest written traces of Spanish language culture appear in documented records?
What is the ISO 639-3 code used internationally to identify the Spanish language?
What phonetic feature characterizes the velar fricative sound represented by the letter 'j' in Spanish?
Among the world's most spoken languages, what rank does Spanish hold globally?
How many total Spanish speakers, including native and second-language speakers, exist worldwide?
Approximately what percentage of the United States population speaks Spanish at home?
In medieval times, before the term 'castellano' became standard, Spanish was referred to by what broader category name?
The ISO 639-1 language code for Spanish is derived from which language's designation?
Which West Iberian Romance language is most closely related to Spanish in terms of linguistic structure and vocabulary?
The palatalization process that transformed the Latin geminate 'nn' into Spanish 'ñ' is exemplified in which word pair?
Approximately how many native Spanish speakers are there worldwide?

source.notes: Roughly 484-500 million native speakers; ~558 million total speakers including L2.

questions.q: Spanish is the second-most spoken native language in the world. What's #1?
Which language family does Spanish belong to?

source.notes: Specifically the Ibero-Romance branch of Romance languages — descended from Vulgar Latin.

questions.q: Which letter is unique to the Spanish alphabet (vs. English)?

source.notes: The 'eñe' represents the palatal nasal /ɲ/ sound.

questions.q: What is the body that regulates the Spanish language as a recognized standard?

source.notes: Founded in Madrid in 1713; works with the Asociación de Academias to standardize across all Spanish-speaking countries.

questions.q: What is the term for the Castilian Spanish distinction between /θ/ (as in 'cinco') and /s/ ('siete')?

source.notes: Most of Latin America uses 'seseo' — pronouncing both letters as /s/.

questions.q: Which non-Latin alphabet was Spanish's predecessor language Iberian once written in?

source.notes: Pre-Roman Iberian languages had their own scripts; Spanish itself has been written in Latin script throughout its history.
